title: Nepotism

jokes.text: I got West Nile virus. Lost a ton of weight. Then I went back to the lake. I stepped on a piece of glass in the parking lot, which hurt. That got infected, even though I peed on it.
jokes.setup: Michael describing his 'great summer' with progressively worse events
jokes.punchline: The casual revelation that he peed on an infected wound as treatment
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalating structure with Michael's oblivious delivery. The peeing detail is shocking but totally in character - he genuinely thinks this was reasonable medical care.

jokes.text: Saw Inception. Or at least I dreamt I did.
jokes.setup: Michael listing summer activities
jokes.punchline: Playing on the movie's dream-within-dream concept
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay on a topical movie. Economy is perfect but lacks deep character integration.

jokes.text: And J.C. We call him Blorville, because he looks like a black Orville Redenbacher.
jokes.setup: Michael casually mentioning college friends
jokes.punchline: The uncomfortable racial nickname delivered with complete obliviousness
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael insensitive comment delivered with total confidence. Makes audience uncomfortable in the intended way.

jokes.text: Gabe is awesome. He has accomplished so much career-wise and height-wise.
jokes.setup: Erin praising her new boyfriend Gabe
jokes.punchline: Adding 'height-wise' as if being tall is an accomplishment parallel to career success
jokes.explanation: Perfect Erin earnestness treating height as a legitimate achievement. Great character voice.

jokes.text: Thank God he's my boss because I would not have said yes to a first date if I didn't have to.
jokes.setup: Erin continuing to praise Gabe
jokes.punchline: Accidentally revealing she only dates him because of workplace power dynamics
jokes.explanation: Brutal honesty delivered with Erin's typical lack of self-awareness. Dark workplace dynamics made funny.

jokes.text: So right now I am sitting on a nice beach at Cape Cod, gazing out across the whitecaps. Oh, look, it's a humpback whale. How pretty. He's eating Gabe.
jokes.setup: Andy describing his anger management visualization technique
jokes.punchline: The serene beach scene suddenly turning violent with Gabe being eaten
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from peaceful to violent, staying in Andy's therapeutic framework but revealing his true feelings.

jokes.text: From now on, if you're hot, sit on an ice pack. Well, what if you're cold? Like you'd ever be cold, Kevin.
jokes.setup: Angela giving temperature instructions to Kevin
jokes.punchline: Her cutting remark about Kevin always being hot/overweight
jokes.explanation: Classic Angela meanness. Predictable fat joke but delivered with perfect Angela disdain.

jokes.text: Hey, Dwight, I don't know if you heard, but we're supposed to be drinking out of weird backpacks instead of cups, like regular people. Oh, you did hear.
jokes.setup: Jim sarcastically commenting on Dwight's hydration pack
jokes.punchline: The visual of Dwight actually wearing a hydration backpack in the office
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim deadpan pointing out Dwight's absurd efficiency obsession. Visual humor works well.

jokes.text: Those of us who are busy require hands-free hydration.
jokes.setup: Dwight defending his hydration pack
jokes.punchline: Taking the concept completely seriously and treating it as business efficiency
jokes.explanation: Pure Dwight logic - turning a silly accessory into business strategy. Perfect character voice.

jokes.text: Then burn the list.
jokes.setup: Dwight listing all his impressive titles and accomplishments
jokes.punchline: Immediately wanting to destroy evidence of his success for mysterious reasons
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and mystery. Classic Dwight paranoia after bragging. The abrupt shift is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: You guys, I'm, like, really smart now. You don't even know. You could ask me, 'Kelly, what's the biggest company in the world?' And I'd be, like, 'Blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah,' giving you the exact right answer.
jokes.setup: Kelly bragging about her education making her smart
jokes.punchline: Demonstrating her intelligence by saying 'blah blah blah' instead of an actual answer
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kelly - completely confident while completely undermining herself. The 'blah blah' is hilariously non-specific.

jokes.text: Tiny. Darryl Hanah.
jokes.setup: Darryl giving nicknames to people
jokes.punchline: Combining 'Daryl Hannah' with his own name as a nickname for someone else
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay on actress name. Works as character moment but not exceptionally funny.

jokes.text: You cool to just drink it, or should I drive all the way back?
jokes.setup: Luke brought decaf instead of regular coffee
jokes.punchline: Acting like it's everyone else's problem and he's doing them a favor
jokes.explanation: Establishes Luke's terrible attitude early. Realistic bad employee behavior that's frustrating but funny.

jokes.text: The new guy sucks. Calls me 'The Nard man.' I'm the 'Nard dog,' okay? Nard man is my father.
jokes.setup: Andy complaining about Luke
jokes.punchline: Getting upset about the wrong version of his ridiculous nickname and invoking his father
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy absurdity - creating a family hierarchy of ridiculous nicknames. The seriousness makes it funnier.

jokes.text: My favorite movies are Citizen Kane and The Boondock Saints.
jokes.setup: Luke introducing himself as someone who loves cinema
jokes.punchline: Pairing a cinematic masterpiece with a cult action movie as equals
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment showing Luke's pretentious but unsophisticated taste. Relatable film snob behavior.

jokes.text: The bigger the key chain, the more powerful the man. That's right. The janitor said that.
jokes.setup: Dwight stating a power principle with confidence
jokes.punchline: Revealing his source of wisdom is the janitor
jokes.explanation: Great subversion - Dwight treats janitor wisdom as profound business insight. Perfect character logic.

jokes.text: I wouldn't know for sure 'cause I don't have a trained ear. That's why I have to use one of these.
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining he can't hear raccoons properly
jokes.punchline: Pulling out an old-fashioned hearing device/stethoscope for raccoon detection
jokes.explanation: Visual gag showing Dwight's over-preparation for minor problems. Good physical comedy setup.

jokes.text: I was just gonna put a couple keys on every day until Christmas. Then his pants would have fallen down, which was a little gift to me, but...
jokes.setup: Pam explaining her long-term prank plan
jokes.punchline: Admitting the pants-falling payoff was selfish motivation disguised as a gift
jokes.explanation: Good prank explanation with honest selfish motivation. Shows Pam's mischievous side emerging.

jokes.text: So unprofessional, Mrs. Halpert.
jokes.setup: Dwight criticizing Pam's prank behavior
jokes.punchline: Calling her by married name while he's the one with a hydration backpack and raccoon-hunting equipment
jokes.explanation: Good irony with Dwight lecturing about professionalism. The formal address adds to the absurdity.

jokes.text: Yeah, strawberries are ripe.
jokes.setup: Luke being asked about work he failed to do
jokes.punchline: Completely ignoring the question to make an irrelevant observation
jokes.explanation: Shows Luke's complete disengagement from work. Random comment highlights his uselessness but isn't particularly clever.

jokes.text: Turn off the Chumbawamba and scram, twerp.
jokes.setup: Meredith addressing Luke's music
jokes.punchline: Using 'Chumbawamba' as generic term for annoying music
jokes.explanation: Good Meredith voice - crude but specific. The outdated band reference is perfectly her.

jokes.text: It's national ice cream afternoon.
jokes.setup: Michael sending Luke to get ice cream
jokes.punchline: Making up a fake holiday to justify the task
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael invention of authority. The specificity of 'afternoon' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: The last time I saw Luke was the opening day of Ace Ventura II, and that was '95. So, yes, 15 years on the dot.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining when he last saw his nephew
jokes.punchline: Using a terrible movie premiere as a significant life timestamp
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael reference point - treating a bad movie opening as a meaningful date. Good character detail.

jokes.text: Anyway, I lost him in a forest.
jokes.setup: Michael recounting the last time he saw Luke
jokes.punchline: Casually mentioning he literally lost a child in the woods
jokes.explanation: Brilliant deadpan delivery of something terrible. Perfect economy and explains the family estrangement. Dark but funny.

jokes.text: Don't don't bother Luke. Got it.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to create a clear sign about Luke
jokes.punchline: The double negative in his correction makes the sign say the opposite of what he wants
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael confusion with grammar creating the opposite meaning. Visual element adds to the confusion.

jokes.text: 'Jo, they're creating a hostile work environment. Stop them.'
jokes.setup: Jim mocking the idea of Gabe tattling
jokes.punchline: Doing a perfect impression of Gabe's whiny, formal complaint style
jokes.explanation: Good character impression showing Jim's ability to nail personalities. Captures Gabe's formal whininess perfectly.

jokes.text: Well, Dwight, he wiped his butt. Is that close enough for you?
jokes.setup: Michael being asked how close he is to Luke
jokes.punchline: Using diaper changing as proof of their relationship bond
jokes.explanation: Michael's gross overshare to prove family connection. Uncomfortable but in character for his boundary issues.

jokes.text: Or what if they just hired the littlest kid and a 50-year-old guy who was a murderer?
jokes.setup: Michael defending nepotism using the band Hanson as an example
jokes.punchline: Creating an absurd alternative scenario involving a child and a murderer
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd escalation showing Michael's terrible logic. The specificity of '50-year-old murderer' is perfect.

jokes.text: God, when he needed help on earth, who did he hire? Jesus Christ, his son.
jokes.setup: Michael continuing to defend nepotism
jokes.punchline: Using God and Jesus as the ultimate nepotism example
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Michael logic using ultimate religious authority to justify office nepotism. Bold and funny comparison.

jokes.text: I'm just saying, why does God get to do something that I don't?
jokes.setup: Follow-up to the God/Jesus nepotism argument
jokes.punchline: Michael positioning himself as equal to God in this situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael ego on display. The economy and character integration are excellent - he genuinely sees himself as God's peer.

jokes.text: He should not be punished because he is related to me and bad at what he does!
jokes.setup: Michael defending Luke against accusations of reverse nepotism
jokes.punchline: Accidentally admitting Luke is both related to him AND bad at his job
jokes.explanation: Great Michael self-contradiction. He accidentally makes the case against himself while trying to defend Luke.

jokes.text: And I was actually gonna be wearing jeans. And I'd point to them, right?
jokes.setup: Michael explaining his planned 'it's in the genes' joke
jokes.punchline: The physical component of pointing to his jeans for the genes/jeans pun
jokes.explanation: Typical Michael bad pun with elaborate physical setup. The over-planning makes it funny even though the pun is terrible.

jokes.text: Soy ice cream? Did you get real ice cream? Or enough for everyone? No, man. There was no list. But I got bagel chips.
jokes.setup: Luke returning from the ice cream run
jokes.punchline: Revealing he got the wrong thing, wrong amount, and his excuse plus terrible alternative
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Luke's incompetence. The bagel chips as ice cream substitute is particularly absurd.

jokes.text: Oh, my favorite part of an ice cream party. The bagel chips.
jokes.setup: Luke's bagel chips substitution for ice cream
jokes.punchline: Jim's perfectly dry sarcastic response
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim sarcasm with great economy. The deadpan delivery makes the absurdity funnier.

jokes.text: Clutch cream run, bro.
jokes.setup: Luke's failed ice cream mission
jokes.punchline: Andy's sarcastic praise using dudebro language
jokes.explanation: Good Andy voice using sarcastic bro language. Economy is excellent but not particularly original.

jokes.text: I don't know if you can tell, but he's mildly upset.
jokes.setup: Jim being obviously frustrated about his ruined prank
jokes.punchline: Pam's massive understatement of Jim's emotional state
jokes.explanation: Good understatement humor. Works as character moment showing Pam reading Jim's subtle reactions.

jokes.text: This is like The Blair Witch Hunt Project.
jokes.setup: Michael defending Luke from investigation
jokes.punchline: Mangling the movie title 'Blair Witch Project' into 'Blair Witch Hunt Project'
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael malapropism. Predictable type of error but fits character perfectly.

jokes.text: I'm reconsidering sending them back anyway.
jokes.setup: Discovery of serious work neglect in Luke's car
jokes.punchline: Phyllis focused on her pants return while everyone else deals with the crisis
jokes.explanation: Perfect Phyllis priorities - completely ignoring the crisis to focus on her personal shopping needs.

jokes.text: Lower yourself, Gabe. I don't wanna be having a conversation with your crotch.
jokes.setup: Jo talking to the very tall Gabe
jokes.punchline: Direct acknowledgment of the height difference in crude terms
jokes.explanation: Good visual callback to Gabe's height with Jo's direct, crude business manner. Works as character moment.

jokes.text: You know why? 'Cause he's a screw-up. He can swim in my pool, but he can't come in my house.
jokes.setup: Jo explaining why her nephew doesn't work for her
jokes.punchline: Creating a specific family boundary system with pool vs house access
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment showing Jo's practical boundaries. The pool/house distinction is perfectly specific and logical.

jokes.text: Well, this office is my pool and my house is my house, and I just want my nephew to work in my pool.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to apply Jo's pool/house metaphor
jokes.punchline: Creating a confused metaphor that doesn't actually make logical sense
jokes.explanation: Michael trying to be clever with Jo's metaphor but getting confused. Shows his inability to match her logic.

jokes.text: I will cover his ass like moss on a Mississippi tree stump.
jokes.setup: Michael promising to protect Luke using Jo's 'tighty-whiteys' metaphor
jokes.punchline: Creating his own unnecessarily elaborate Southern metaphor instead
jokes.explanation: Michael's attempt to out-folksy Jo with an even more elaborate metaphor. The specificity is funny but overwrought.

jokes.text: Okay, that's weird. I was just hitting 'door close.' What? There we go. Well... Good.
jokes.setup: Pam's elevator prank begins affecting Dwight
jokes.punchline: Dwight's confused reaction to the elevator doing the opposite of what he wants
jokes.explanation: Start of physical prank payoff. Dwight's confusion is in character but not exceptionally funny on its own.

jokes.text: I got 56 ounces of fluid in my bladder! And we have to establish a pee corner.
jokes.setup: Dwight thinking they're trapped in elevator
jokes.punchline: Immediately jumping to primitive survival mode with specific bladder capacity and territorial planning
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight panic response - immediate survival planning with grossly specific details. The precision of '56 ounces' is brilliant.

jokes.text: Not on Oscar's head. All right. Luke, come on. Stop it. What? I'm stopping it. That's what you said.
jokes.setup: Luke using laser pointer inappropriately during presentation
jokes.punchline: Luke's literal interpretation of 'stop it' while continuing to be disruptive
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy with Luke's defiant incompetence. Shows his terrible attitude but isn't particularly clever.

jokes.text: I am going to count down from five. Five. Five. Four. Four. I'm not kidding. Three. Three. Two. Two. One. Give... Okay.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to assert authority over Luke
jokes.punchline: Luke mockingly repeating each number, completely undermining Michael's authority
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Michael's complete loss of authority. Luke's mockery is painfully effective.

jokes.text: You are going to learn to be more professional, Luke! [SPANKING SOUNDS] That's what you're going to do, Luke!
jokes.setup: Michael's complete loss of control with Luke
jokes.punchline: Michael actually spanking his adult nephew in front of the entire office
jokes.explanation: Shocking escalation that's both horrifying and funny. Perfect character breakdown for Michael - completely inappropriate but understandable given his frustration.

jokes.text: That was awesome. Texas justice.
jokes.setup: Everyone's shocked reaction to the spanking
jokes.punchline: Creed's enthusiastic approval with regional justice terminology
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed response - completely inappropriate enthusiasm for workplace violence. Great economy and character voice.

jokes.text: Finally, a kid who's not talking about Betty White.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Luke's Twitter complaints
jokes.punchline: Stanley's appreciation for any content that isn't about the trendy celebrity topic
jokes.explanation: Good Stanley grumpiness about pop culture trends. Feels authentically curmudgeonly and specific to the era.

jokes.text: Well, you know, they don't call me 'The Bart Simpson of Scranton' for nothing. Do they call you that? They do call me that.
jokes.setup: Pam bragging about her prank success
jokes.punchline: Jim's questioning followed by Pam's defensive confirmation of her made-up nickname
jokes.explanation: Good setup/payoff showing Pam's pride in her pranking abilities. The self-appointed nickname is perfectly awkward.

jokes.text: Also, there's pee on the floor. Oh. Of course there is.
jokes.setup: Jim offering to help Pam down from the elevator ceiling
jokes.punchline: Callback to Dwight's earlier bladder panic and Jim's resigned acceptance
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier Dwight panic. Jim's weary acceptance is perfectly in character for dealing with Dwight's extremes.

jokes.text: I don't know how Ringling Brothers do it. Night after night, town after town, all across America. You would think they'd be sick of each other by now, but, clearly, they make it work.
jokes.setup: Michael reflecting on working with family after firing Luke
jokes.punchline: Using a traveling circus as his model for successful family business relationships
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael reference point - using circus performers as his business inspiration. The earnestness makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm really looking forward to working with you, Michael.
jokes.setup: Michael learning Toby will be his counselor
jokes.punchline: Toby's fake enthusiasm for the terrible situation he's been put in
jokes.explanation: Good setup for future torture. Toby's sarcastic enthusiasm knowing how much Michael hates him makes this darkly funny.

tmdb_overview: Michael declines to fire an unpopular new assistant; Andy plays it cool around Erin while Pam attempts an office prank.
